claim: fill()'s new inner read loop no longer returns as soon as a complete line is available; reads until buffer full or EOF.
No regression test exercises the short-read amortization under large -A; all Read-path tests use &[u8] that never short-reads.
Comment above binary3 golden literal not extended to explain the new 262142 byte-count provenance.
roll() comment omits the invariant that makes before_context (not max_context) correct: after-context re-emitted via after_context_left.
Inner read-loop in fill() has no comment explaining why it must fill the whole buffer to amortize per-fill work.
In BinaryDetection::Quit mode the inner loop drains the entire buffer before binary detection, over-reading past a NUL.
fill()'s new inner read loop blocks until buffer full or EOF, silently defeating --line-buffered/streaming stdin (tail -f, journalctl -f).
No regression test exercises fill()'s incremental/streaming return behavior.
Inner loop fills the entire free buffer before checking for a line terminator, delaying output for line-buffered/interactive stdin.
Validator confirmed: fill()'s inner read loop withholds output until buffer full/EOF, breaking --line-buffered / streaming stdin.
Validator confirmed: roll() comment never explains why after-context needs no backward preservation.
Validator confirmed: worst-case over-read before a Quit byte is bounded by buffer size, not a single read().
Inner read loop propagates io::ErrorKind::Interrupted via ? instead of retrying, unlike the crate's other read-until-full loop.
New inner read-loop has no in-code rationale; the amortization reason lives only in the commit message.
fill()'s inner loop only returns once the free buffer is full or EOF, delaying output for slow/streaming stdin by up to a buffer's worth.
Validator (High): fill()'s inner loop blocks until buffer full/EOF instead of returning after the first usable read, breaking --line-buffered streaming.
New read loop binds read() to readlen; both other workspace read sites name it nread.
No test exercises the new multi-short-read aggregation in LineBuffer::fill.
Changed golden byte-counts (262142) are undocumented magic numbers with no derivation comment.
Inner read-loop has no in-code rationale, so a maintainer could simplify it away and silently reintroduce #3184.
The blocking-until-full streaming tradeoff is documented nowhere in code or comments.
roll() comment records the decision but not the correctness invariant; 'skip this step' is imprecise.
Inner read-loop turns fill() from 'return on first usable chunk' to 'block until buffer full or EOF', breaking incremental/streaming stdin.
In Quit binary mode the loop reads far past the binary marker before detecting it, wasted/blocking I/O.
No test exercises aggregating multiple short reads before returning.

New fill() inner loop reads until buffer full/EOF and never breaks on an available line terminator, regressing streaming/interactive latency (tail -f, kubectl logs -f).
Narrowing preceding() to before_context makes roll() correctness rest on an implicit, unguarded invariant not stated in the comment.
The fill-to-capacity loop's intent is undocumented, inviting a simplification that silently reintroduces #3184.
Changelog pairs issue #3184 with a /pull/ URL; fix landed as PR #3185.
Comment says the costly step is skipped when before_context==0, but there is no skip branch; preceding() always runs.
Read result bound as readlen, but every other manual read-loop in the crate uses nread.
roll() change unguarded: all 77 tests pass when reverted; no test covers before_context=0 + after_context>0 + haystack >64KB spanning a roll.
Redundant same-name rebind 'let consumed=...; consumed' (pre-existing).
Inner while loop defers binary-detection and line-terminator search until the whole free buffer fills; real regression for streaming/interactive stdin.
New inner fill-loop short-read accumulation path unexercised by every test.
On a low-rate interactive stream the loop keeps calling read() until the buffer is full, so live-follow output stalls indefinitely.
Reducing roll lookback to before_context makes correctness depend on an implicit, unguarded invariant with no debug_assert.
Entry pairs issue #3184 with a /pull/ URL.
fill() inner loop deliberately fills the whole buffer but nothing says why; a maintainer could simplify it and reintroduce #3184.

No test guards the roll() before_context-vs-max_context() change; reverting core.rs leaves all 77 tests passing.
No regression test for the roll() before_context/max_context change across a buffer roll with large -A.
Reader returning data then transient Ok(0) then more data with no line terminator accumulates the whole stream into one ever-growing buffer (OOM).
fill() drains until buffer full/EOF, silently changing the blocking/latency contract of the public search_reader streaming path.
fill() blocks until the whole 64KB buffer fills or EOF, not stopping at a line-terminator boundary.
[Inference] Packing the buffer to 64KB before returning introduces a streaming/interactive-stdin latency regression.
EINTR surfaces as a hard error rather than being retried; pre-existing, not a regression.
After-context could be dropped or duplicated across a buffer roll with no multi-buffer + -A test to catch it.

before/after/max_context() are three interchangeable bare usize, mutually substitutable — the design that caused this bug.

No regression test for the actual bug — large -A/-B over an incremental/stdin reader.
EOF/error mid-accumulation inside the inner loop is untested; loop doesn't retry Interrupted.
Comment 'skip this step when before_context==0' doesn't match code; preceding is unconditional and preceding(..,0) still reverse-scans.
end<=buf.len() relies on Read honoring its contract; a pathological Read trips the slice bound (safe panic); type doesn't defend it.
before/after/max_context() three interchangeable usize; wrong one type-checked and produced correct output, surfacing only as perf regression.

PR adds no new test functions; large -A with -B 0 across multiple buffer cycles and stdin amortization have no regression test.
bytes_searched / --stats output silently changes for binary-quit inputs and is undocumented in the CHANGELOG.
Inner loop advances self.end after each read, so a later read error in the same pass propagates before already-read bytes are searched, discarding matches.

fill() inner loop has no early exit on a line terminator; slow non-EOF stdin producers buffer up to 64KB before any output.
self.end advanced after each read but a subsequent read error propagates before bytes are searched; caller aborts and never re-reads, dropping matched input.
fill() now loops read() until the free buffer is exhausted or EOF instead of returning after a single read, delaying already-available data.
